urn:contentItem:6JJG-G2H3-S0TG-H17J-00000-00Weekly: DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) in bottom 8% performers of S&P 500 Index in past week; -$8.19 [-5.5%] Vol Index 1.9 [1 is avg]2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:55:37ZLexisNexisWeekly: DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) in bottom 8% performers of S&P 500 Index in past week; -$8.19 [-5.5%] Vol Index 1.9 [1 is avg]

NYSE END-OF-WEEK STOCK REPORT

www.buysellsignals.com

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE $140.60), the NYSE's 8th largest Conventional electricity company by market cap, has fell $8.19 (or 5.5%) in the past week to close at $140.60.

This makes it the thirty-first lowest performer in the S&P 500 Index which was up 2.3% in the past week; a relative price change of -7.8%.

During the week its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 5.8% was 2 times its average weekly volatility of 3.6%.

In the past week the market cap has declined by $1.7 billion to $29.2 billion.

This has been exacerbated by high volume of 1.9 times average for the week.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a diversified energy firm focusing on electric and natural gas services, primarily in Michigan. Key operations include DTE Electric, offering customers dependable power, and energy efficiency programs that enhance operational performance. DTE Energy is advancing in renewable energy with solar and wind projects and actively supports commercial solar initiatives. Its innovation pipeline includes smart grid modernization and battery storage, increasing energy management capabilities. Long-term competitive strengths are seen in its substantial investment in clean energy and DTE's commitment to community sustainability, ensuring robust corporate social responsibility through DTE Energy Foundation and sponsorships.

Quarterly Report (Q1/2026):

Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: DTE Energy sequential quarterly profits hit by 64% fall

DTE Energy (NYSE:DTE), announced net profit of $247m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], down 64% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and down 44% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was down 44.4%, Revenue was up 15.8% and Net Profit was down 44.5%.

Friday's price drop

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) in 6th consecutive fall

DETROIT, Friday, May 08 2026 - DTE Energy Company's (NYSE: DTE $140.57) stock price slipped further on Friday, sliding an additional $1.43 (1.0%) to close at $140.57. This brought its 6-day fall to 7.3%; compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 187.6 points (2.6%) in the six days for a relative price change of -9.9%. The stock decreased 0.7% on Wednesday and 0.3% on Thursday. The fall was exacerbated by a cumulative 6-day volume of 1.8 times the average 6-day volume of 1.4 million shares. There were 1,279,125 shares worth $179.8 million traded today.

SECTION 6 CORPORATE PROFILE

6.1 Activities

DTE Energy Company, headquartered in Detroit, Michigan, is a diversified energy utility serving millions of customers across the Midwest United States. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through subsidiaries like DTE Electric and DTE Gas. The company operates a mix of energy sources, including coal, nuclear, and renewables, while focusing on reliable infrastructure and customer services for residential, commercial, and industrial sectors.

Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy projects, such as wind and solar developments, which have expanded rapidly to meet rising demand for clean energy and regulatory mandates. Additionally, DTE Energy's non-utility operations, including energy trading and infrastructure services, are gaining momentum through investments in electric vehicle charging networks and advanced energy storage solutions. This strategic shift underscores the company's commitment to sustainability and long-term growth in a dynamic energy market. It is the NYSE's 8th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a prominent player in the energy sector, offering a comprehensive range of services including DTE Energy electricity service and DTE Energy natural gas service. With a strong focus on sustainability, DTE Energy renewable energy initiatives are spearheaded by its DTE Energy solar programs and DTE Energy wind projects. The company is a leader in DTE Energy EV charging stations and is committed to DTE Energy grid modernization through innovations like DTE Energy smart meters and DTE Energy battery energy storage. Customers benefit from DTE Energy energy efficiency programs and a variety of DTE Energy rate plans. The company’s investment in DTE Energy community solar and DTE Energy commercial energy solutions aligns with its DTE Energy net zero strategy and DTE Energy carbon reduction goals. DTE Energy corporate sponsorship, including DTE sponsorship City Opera House, highlights its robust DTE Energy community partnerships, reinforcing DTE Energy utility services Michigan. DTE Energy investor NYSE:DTE supports its growth trajectory through DTE Energy business energy management and DTE Energy rebates and incentives.

value.Title: Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban

Kathiann M. Kowalski, Canary Media

May 12th, 2026 ( Ohio Capital Journal - Delivered by Newstex )

Graham Diedrich, a Ph.D. student at the University of Michigan, surveys voters outside a polling location in Richland County, Ohio, on May 5, 2026, after they cast their ballots on the county's renewable energy ban. (Photo by Kathiann M. Kowalski, Canary Media.)

This story was originally published by Canary Media.

Confusing ballot language could be the reason an Ohio county upheld aban on renewable energy lastweek.

An early analysis of exit poll responses suggests amajority of voters likely meant to vote against Richland County's ban on most large solar and wind projects for11of its18townships. But the ballot's wordingperplexed enough of them to have flipped the results.

That preliminary finding doesn't change the outcome of the May5Richland County election. The final tallywas53%​'yes' votes to keep the ban versus47%​'no' votes to axe it. But the poll sheds light on how people in the county really felt and can inform future work to roll back clean energy restrictions in Ohio and beyond.

Richland County's referendum drew national attention because it was arare case of residents pushing back against limits on solar and wind power. Such state and local restrictions have grown dramaticallyacross the U.S. in recent years.

Meanwhile, skyrocketing demand for electricity is fueling an affordability crisis. Solar and wind, and batteries to store their energy, can generally come online more quicklythan natural gas plants to meet some of that demand. Renewables also aren't subject to fluctuating fuel costs, and they increase competition in electricity markets, which can rein in power prices.

Richland County's three commissioners relied on a2021state law, Senate Bill52, to pass the restrictions last July. Local residents who opposed the ban quickly began pushing to put it to avote.SB52states that for any referendum against acounty's ban, its commissioners' resolution needs amajority vote in favorto go into effect.

So, a​'yes' vote means someone opposes the referendum effort to overturn the ban, while a​'no' vote means the person backs the campaign to get rid of theban.

'It's confusing,' County Commissioner Cliff Mears told Canary Media in March when explaining his support against the referendum and for theban.

Lots of voters may have been confused, too - about one in five across all political groups, according to early analysis of the exit surveys completed by1,193of the23,042people who voted on the issue.​'When we model what the result would have looked like if everyone had voted their stated preference, the outcome flips,' to54% wanting to reverse the ban and46% wanting to keep it, said Graham Diedrich, aUniversity of Michigan Ph.D. candidate who oversaw the exit polling at adozen locations across Richland County.

A bar chart from Graham Diedrich shows how results on the Richland County referendum may have flipped if the ballot language hadn't confused voters. (Graham Diedrich)

'We anticipated this would be an issue,' said Bella Bogin, director of programs for Ohio Citizen Action, an organizing group that assisted Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development with the​'no' campaign.​'I think we did the best we could on educating folks on this very complicated ballot language.'

Misunderstanding went beyond the ballot language, said Brian McPeek, one of the local leaders for the vote-no group. Those in favor of the ban suggested that repealing it would open the floodgates for projects to come into the area, McPeek said. In fact, the county would simply have returned to the prior system of accepting or rejecting most new solar and wind farms on acase-by-case basis before the projects head to the Ohio Power Siting Board for state permitting.

To further complicate matters, supporters of the renewable energy restrictions tried to cast the referendum effort as driven by outside interests, pointing to the New York headquarters for theNRDCAction Fund, which provided advertising for the campaign.

However, all decision-making for the campaign was handled by local leaders, stressed Bogin at Ohio Citizen Action. People from Ohio Citizen Action and theNRDCAction Fund identified their organizations at public town hall meetings. And Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development disclosed both groups clearly on its finance report. The finance report for the campaign to maintain the ban didn't highlight its connections to cheerleaders for the natural gas industry, such as The Empowerment Alliance. Canary Mediaand the Energy and Policy Institutepublicized those links only after connecting the dots from other public records and emails.

Bigger picture, Ohio's preference for fossil fuelsis causing renewable energy companies to take many projects elsewhere, said Michael Benson, president of the board of directors for Green Energy Ohio, an industry association.​'We are losing out on the economic development potential solar provides to astate in desperate need of more energy for ourgrid.'

Lessons learned

The campaign against the ban did not explicitly stress renewable energy's role in limiting climate change and its impacts. Yet concern about awarming planet seemed to factor into residents' votes.

'Voters who were very worried about climate change backed repeal at75%, while those not at all worried backed the ban at the same rate,' Diedrich said. Separate responses to a2025survey analyzed by the Yale Program on Climate Change Communication show that 53%of people in Richland County said they were worried about climate change. That's close to the54% of exit-poll respondents whose answers suggested they opposed theban.

Party affiliation was also afactor, with72% of Republicans voting for the ban and76% of Democrats voting against it. However, Richland County is home to far more Republicans, who outnumber Democratsby more than3to1, meaning many must have voted against the ban in order for the final results to be soclose.

'One of the biggest lessons from this campaign is just how important sustained local organizing and trusted community voices are in conversations around energy and land use,' Bogin said. In her view, the movement to reverse the ban in Richland County got as much support as it did​'because the people making the case were neighbors talking to neighbors.' She remains hopeful that other efforts to reverse prohibitions on clean energy may succeed, and thinks the campaign could trigger alarger discussion at the statehouse about the hurdlesSB52creates for renewables, which don't apply to either fossil fuelor nuclear projects.

Although the campaign did not overturn the ban, Diedrich noted that90% of survey respondents were aware of the renewables referendum before casting their ballot. To him, that shows the group effectively educated voters, despite the​'structural' problem of the ballot wording.

McPeek said he still feels that most people will ultimately come out against blanket prohibitions on solar and wind when they get all the facts. And adoption of aprohibition against solar and wind does not mean it will go on forever.

'The question of who gets to decide what happens on private land in Richland County did not end tonight,' Morgan Carroll, another leader in the campaign against the ban, said in astatement after the vote was tallied on Election Day.​'We will continue to stand with farmers and landowners who believe that right belongs to them, not to their government and certainly not to the fossil fuel industry.'

NYSE END-OF-WEEK STOCK REPORT

www.buysellsignals.com

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE $140.60), the NYSE's 8th largest Conventional electricity company by market cap, has fell $8.19 (or 5.5%) in the past week to close at $140.60.

This makes it the thirty-first lowest performer in the S&P 500 Index which was up 2.3% in the past week; a relative price change of -7.8%.

During the week its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 5.8% was 2 times its average weekly volatility of 3.6%.

In the past week the market cap has declined by $1.7 billion to $29.2 billion.

This has been exacerbated by high volume of 1.9 times average for the week.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a diversified energy firm focusing on electric and natural gas services, primarily in Michigan. Key operations include DTE Electric, offering customers dependable power, and energy efficiency programs that enhance operational performance. DTE Energy is advancing in renewable energy with solar and wind projects and actively supports commercial solar initiatives. Its innovation pipeline includes smart grid modernization and battery storage, increasing energy management capabilities. Long-term competitive strengths are seen in its substantial investment in clean energy and DTE's commitment to community sustainability, ensuring robust corporate social responsibility through DTE Energy Foundation and sponsorships.

Friday's price drop

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) in 6th consecutive fall

DETROIT, Friday, May 08 2026 - DTE Energy Company's (NYSE: DTE $140.57) stock price slipped further on Friday, sliding an additional $1.43 (1.0%) to close at $140.57. This brought its 6-day fall to 7.3%; compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 187.6 points (2.6%) in the six days for a relative price change of -9.9%. The stock decreased 0.7% on Wednesday and 0.3% on Thursday. The fall was exacerbated by a cumulative 6-day volume of 1.8 times the average 6-day volume of 1.4 million shares. May 07 2026: DTE Energy's Bold Move: Targeting 8.4 GW Data Center Demand

Detroit-based DTE Energy is positioning itself to support a surge in data center development, identifying up to 8.4 GW of potential load growth tied to major technology clients and future projects, company officials said during a recent earnings call. This announcement comes as DTE Energy has already begun construction on a 1.4-GW data center for Oracle, while regulatory approval is being sought for a separate 1-GW facility for Google.

DTE Energy President and CEO Joi Harris highlighted the utility's steady progress in executing and finalizing the necessary contractual agreements to support this anticipated data center growth. Despite reporting a decline in first-quarter 2026 operating earnings to $407 million from $436 million the previous year, DTE's electric utility segment showed resilience, with operating earnings rising to $218 million from $147 million in the same period last year.

The utility received conditional approval from the Michigan Public Service Commission in December for its agreement with Oracle, with energy demand expected to increase over the coming years. For the Google project, which is anticipated to reach full load by 2028, DTE Energy is awaiting regulatory approval, with plans for a combination of renewable energy and storage resources. This project could catalyze approximately $5 billion in additional investment in generation and storage infrastructure through 2032.

In addition to these major projects, DTE Energy is engaged in advanced discussions with other data center developments, representing around 2 GW of potential load, with another 3 GW to 4 GW in earlier-stage opportunities. These initiatives are expected to require substantial investments in new baseload generation, renewables, and related storage, contributing to DTE's long-term earnings growth target of 6% to 8% annually through 2030.

Moreover, DTE Energy is committing approximately $11 billion over five years to enhance grid reliability, which includes deploying smart grid technologies. Following a 2024 audit that criticized outage response times, DTE has made notable progress, reducing outages by 60% during severe weather events compared to previous years. The utility is also pursuing a $474.3 million rate case, primarily focused on infrastructure upgrades, while promising to freeze rates for two years, contingent on meeting specific project milestones.

SECTION 9 CORPORATE PROFILE

9.1 Activities

DTE Energy Company, headquartered in Detroit, Michigan, is a diversified energy utility serving millions of customers across the Midwest United States. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through subsidiaries like DTE Electric and DTE Gas. The company operates a mix of energy sources, including coal, nuclear, and renewables, while focusing on reliable infrastructure and customer services for residential, commercial, and industrial sectors.

Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy projects, such as wind and solar developments, which have expanded rapidly to meet rising demand for clean energy and regulatory mandates. Additionally, DTE Energy's non-utility operations, including energy trading and infrastructure services, are gaining momentum through investments in electric vehicle charging networks and advanced energy storage solutions. This strategic shift underscores the company's commitment to sustainability and long-term growth in a dynamic energy market. It is the NYSE's 8th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a prominent player in the energy sector, offering a comprehensive range of services including DTE Energy electricity service and DTE Energy natural gas service. With a strong focus on sustainability, DTE Energy renewable energy initiatives are spearheaded by its DTE Energy solar programs and DTE Energy wind projects. The company is a leader in DTE Energy EV charging stations and is committed to DTE Energy grid modernization through innovations like DTE Energy smart meters and DTE Energy battery energy storage. Customers benefit from DTE Energy energy efficiency programs and a variety of DTE Energy rate plans. The company’s investment in DTE Energy community solar and DTE Energy commercial energy solutions aligns with its DTE Energy net zero strategy and DTE Energy carbon reduction goals. DTE Energy corporate sponsorship, including DTE sponsorship City Opera House, highlights its robust DTE Energy community partnerships, reinforcing DTE Energy utility services Michigan. DTE Energy investor NYSE:DTE supports its growth trajectory through DTE Energy business energy management and DTE Energy rebates and incentives.

DETROIT, Friday, May 08 2026 - DTE Energy Company's (NYSE: DTE $140.57) stock price slipped further on Friday, sliding an additional $1.43 (1.0%) to close at $140.57.

This brought its 6-day fall to 7.3%; compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 187.6 points (2.6%) in the six days for a relative price change of -9.9%. The stock decreased 0.7% on Wednesday and 0.3% on Thursday.

The fall was exacerbated by a cumulative 6-day volume of 1.8 times the average 6-day volume of 1.4 million shares. There were 1,279,125 shares worth $179.8 million traded today.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a diversified energy firm focusing on electric and natural gas services, primarily in Michigan. Key operations include DTE Electric, offering customers dependable power, and energy efficiency programs that enhance operational performance. DTE Energy is advancing in renewable energy with solar and wind projects and actively supports commercial solar initiatives. Its innovation pipeline includes smart grid modernization and battery storage, increasing energy management capabilities. Long-term competitive strengths are seen in its substantial investment in clean energy and DTE's commitment to community sustainability, ensuring robust corporate social responsibility through DTE Energy Foundation and sponsorships.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

DTE Energy Company, headquartered in Detroit, Michigan, is a diversified energy utility serving millions of customers across the Midwest United States. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through subsidiaries like DTE Electric and DTE Gas. The company operates a mix of energy sources, including coal, nuclear, and renewables, while focusing on reliable infrastructure and customer services for residential, commercial, and industrial sectors.

Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy projects, such as wind and solar developments, which have expanded rapidly to meet rising demand for clean energy and regulatory mandates. Additionally, DTE Energy's non-utility operations, including energy trading and infrastructure services, are gaining momentum through investments in electric vehicle charging networks and advanced energy storage solutions. This strategic shift underscores the company's commitment to sustainability and long-term growth in a dynamic energy market. It is the NYSE's 8th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) is a prominent player in the energy sector, offering a comprehensive range of services including DTE Energy electricity service and DTE Energy natural gas service. With a strong focus on sustainability, DTE Energy renewable energy initiatives are spearheaded by its DTE Energy solar programs and DTE Energy wind projects. The company is a leader in DTE Energy EV charging stations and is committed to DTE Energy grid modernization through innovations like DTE Energy smart meters and DTE Energy battery energy storage. Customers benefit from DTE Energy energy efficiency programs and a variety of DTE Energy rate plans. The company’s investment in DTE Energy community solar and DTE Energy commercial energy solutions aligns with its DTE Energy net zero strategy and DTE Energy carbon reduction goals. DTE Energy corporate sponsorship, including DTE sponsorship City Opera House, highlights its robust DTE Energy community partnerships, reinforcing DTE Energy utility services Michigan. DTE Energy investor NYSE:DTE supports its growth trajectory through DTE Energy business energy management and DTE Energy rebates and incentives.

SECTION 3 RECENT NEWS, PAST YEAR TOP 10 STORIES & PAST MONTH ECONOMIC NEWS

3.1 RECENT NEWS

Recent News 1 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 394 words

Fri May 1, 2026: DTE Energy Unveils 8.4 GW Data Center Growth Potential

DTE Energy Company is poised to capitalize on significant growth in the data center sector, with plans to support a 1.4-GW Oracle data center currently under construction. On the 1st of May, 2026, company officials revealed that they are also in discussions regarding an additional 7 GW of potential data center projects, indicating a robust pipeline that could reshape the utility's operations in the coming years.

During the company's first quarter earnings call, President and CEO Joi Harris noted that DTE Energy is making steady progress in finalizing contractual agreements to support this data center growth. The utility reported operating earnings of $407 million for Q1 2026, a slight decrease from $436 million in the same period of 2025, attributed to fluctuations in energy trading, tax timing, and elevated interest expenses. However, its electric subsidiary, DTE Electric, experienced an increase in operating earnings to $218 million, up from $147 million year-over-year, driven by favorable weather conditions and successful rate implementations.

The Michigan Public Service Commission granted conditional approval for the Oracle contract in December, paving the way for construction and phased load ramp-up over the next several years. Oracle's investment includes covering the full cost of its energy and capacity needs, supported by existing capabilities and planned energy storage. In addition, DTE Energy is awaiting approval for a 1-GW Google data center project, which is expected to come online by the end of 2028, bolstered by a mix of renewable generation and energy storage solutions.

Meeting the energy demands of Google could drive an estimated $5 billion in incremental investments in generation and storage through 2032. Harris emphasized that these investments are structured to protect existing customers while providing affordability benefits. The proposed 20-year power supply agreement with Google includes provisions designed to ensure that current customers are not adversely affected by the utility's expansion efforts.

Beyond the Oracle and Google projects, DTE Energy is in advanced discussions concerning additional data center projects that could add 2 GW of incremental load, with even more potential projects identified in the pipeline. As the utility continues to enhance its electric reliability—evidenced by a recent audit and a commitment to modernize its distribution system—DTE Energy remains confident in its ability to meet its long-term operating earnings per share growth target of 6-8% through 2030, supported by ongoing investments and strategic partnerships in the evolving data center landscape.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 359 words

Tue Apr 28, 2026: DTE Energy's Rate Freeze Plan Tied to Oracle's Data Centre Development

US utility DTE Energy has announced a plan to pause customer rate increases for two years, contingent upon the successful development of a US$16 billion data centre campus being constructed for tech giant Oracle. This announcement comes as DTE Energy prepares to file for regulatory approval with the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) on 28th April 2026, aiming for a rate freeze that extends through at least 2028.

Joi Harris, president and CEO of DTE Energy, emphasized the importance of the data centre project, stating, 'As long as the first data centre project we're supporting comes online as planned by the end of 2027 and we're able to receive other regulatory approvals, we will refrain from filing another rate request until at least 2028—providing customers two years without an increase in rates after the current request is complete.'

The two data centre contracts associated with the Oracle project are projected to contribute approximately US$9 billion to enhancements in DTE's electric system by 2045, thereby alleviating cost pressures on other customers. The utility's upcoming request for US$474.3 million will support significant investments in the electric grid and power generation, including the conversion of the Belle River Power Plant from coal to natural gas and the establishment of the 220MW/880MWh Trenton Channel Energy Center, which is expected to become the largest stand-alone battery energy storage facility in the Great Lakes region upon completion.

DTE Energy's construction of the battery energy storage system (BESS) commenced in 2024, following MPSC approval for the Trenton project. The utility had originally partnered with Powin for the BESS technology but shifted to LG Energy Solution after experiencing delays and a contract termination with Powin in June 2025. This strategic pivot is aimed at achieving a cost reduction of over US$30 million for the project.

The financing for Related Digital's data centre, which will utilize DTE Energy's power, involves equity from Related Digital and funds associated with Blackstone, alongside long-term fixed-rate debt anchored by Pacific Investment Management Company (PIMCO). The project is anticipated to generate significant economic benefits, including job creation and tax revenue for local communities, while also reinforcing DTE Energy's commitment to renewable energy solutions.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 298 words

Thu Apr 23, 2026: City Opera House Partners with DTE Energy for Sustainable Initiatives

TRAVERSE CITY — The City Opera House has named DTE Energy as its first green sponsor to support environmentally conscious operations. As of 24th April 2026, this partnership marks a significant step towards implementing sustainability initiatives within the organization.

The collaboration aims to enhance eco-friendly practices, including the transition to rechargeable systems for microphone packs, which will eliminate the need for disposable batteries. Additionally, the City Opera House is adopting recycled copy paper throughout its operations, encompassing all printed program inserts. Future plans involve installing a bike rack to promote alternative transportation options for both patrons and staff.

Chad Lindsey, the executive director of the City Opera House, emphasized the importance of this partnership, stating, “DTE Energy's partnership helps us make thoughtful, earth-conscious decisions that align with our values as a historic community anchor. Together, we're ensuring that our operations reflect not only artistic excellence but environmental responsibility.” This sentiment underscores the venue's commitment to integrating sustainability into its core mission.

Henry Decker, vice president of gas sales and supply at DTE Energy, reiterated the company's dedication to sustainability, remarking, “We look forward to supporting the City Opera House as they continue to make a meaningful difference for the environment and for the people they serve.” DTE Energy is known for its initiatives that help customers reduce their carbon footprints, such as the CleanVision Natural Gas Balance program, which balances carbon emissions from natural gas use by supporting Michigan forest management and the development of renewable natural gas.

The City Opera House serves as a cultural hub for NorthernMichigan and remains committed to reducing its environmental footprint. The collaboration with DTE Energy is a promising step towards fostering a more sustainable future, reflecting a growing trend among cultural institutions to prioritize environmental responsibility alongside their artistic missions.

3.2 PAST YEAR TOP 10 STORIES

Story 1 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 72 words

Operational Veteran Joi Harris Elected CEO to Navigate Decarbonization and Grid Challenges

Joi Harris, formerly President and COO, has been appointed CEO and President of DTE Energy, succeeding Jerry Norcia. Her extensive operational experience is expected to guide the company through its decarbonization strategy and grid modernization challenges. While her appointment is viewed as a move for continuity, she faces significant regulatory scrutiny over rates and service quality, and her performance will be measured by her ability to meet growth targets amidst these pressures.

Story 2 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 70 words

DTE Energy Outperforms Q3 Expectations Despite Revenue Headwinds, Reaffirms Guidance

DTE Energy reported Q3 2025 operating earnings of $2.25 per share, surpassing estimates despite a nearly $1 billion revenue shortfall caused by lower fuel cost pass-throughs. The earnings beat was driven by cost management and tax credit timing in the DTE Electric segment. Management reaffirmed its full-year guidance, signaling financial stability, but also noted that future growth will depend more on industrial and data center demand rather than residential load.

Story 3 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 74 words

MPSC Rejection of Saline Data Center Contract Signals Regulatory Headwinds for Tech Load Growth

DTE Energy's plan to build a major data center in Saline Township was blocked after the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) failed to approve a special contract before a key deadline. The project was central to DTE's strategy of offsetting stagnant residential demand. The rejection, based on concerns about cost-shifting to residential customers and grid stability, sets a challenging precedent for future large-scale industrial projects and introduces significant execution risk to DTE's growth thesis.

Story 4 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 63 words

Regulator Authorizes $217 Million Rate Increase, Capping Revenue Below Utility Request

The MPSC approved a $217.4 million rate increase for DTE Electric, which was only 48% of the company's original request. While the revenue supports grid modernization and near-term financials, the significant reduction signals a tightening regulatory environment. This outcome forces DTE to file for rate increases more frequently to recover its infrastructure investments, a strategy that risks creating friction with regulators and consumers.

Story 5 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 67 words

DTE Files $574 Million Rate Case Amidst Heightened Political Scrutiny

Shortly after its last rate adjustment, DTE Energy filed for another increase, requesting $574.1 million to fund a $4.4 billion investment in grid hardening and cleaner energy. The request has drawn immediate political opposition, notably from the Michigan Attorney General. The outcome is critical for DTE's ability to earn its authorized return and execute its reliability improvement strategy, but a significant disallowance could threaten its growth targets.

Story 6 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 64 words

Investment Plan Expanded by $6.5 Billion to Support Grid Modernization and Load Growth

DTE Energy has increased its five-year capital investment plan by $6.5 billion to accelerate grid modernization and expand renewable generation. The expansion is intended to support a 6-8% annual EPS growth rate, driven by projected load increases from electrification and data centers. However, this large-scale plan faces significant execution risk, including the need for regulatory approval for cost recovery and potential supply chain constraints.

Story 7 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 58 words

Commissioning of Polaris Solar Park Advances Voluntary Green Power Strategy

DTE Energy has brought the 100-megawatt Polaris Solar Park into commercial operation. As a key part of its MIGreenPower program, the facility allows corporate customers to meet their ESG goals. The project's success demonstrates DTE's ability to execute utility-scale solar developments, although the scalability of this model faces future challenges related to land availability and local community opposition.

Story 8 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 63 words

Construction Begins on Dedicated Solar Array for Ford Motor Company

DTE has started construction on a utility-scale solar park dedicated to supplying power to Ford Motor Company's EV manufacturing operations. This project secures long-term revenue from a major industrial partner and mitigates the risk of load defection. While it reinforces DTE's "energy-as-a-service" model, it also ties a portion of the utility's performance to the cyclical automotive industry and Ford's specific EV strategy success.

Story 9 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 65 words

Conversion of Belle River Plant Commences, Pivoting Coal Assets to Peaker Role

DTE has begun converting a coal-fired unit at its Belle River Power Plant to a natural gas peaker plant. The $154 million project is a strategic step to ensure grid reliability as the company retires coal plants and integrates more renewables. This conversion repurposes an existing asset to maintain resource adequacy cost-effectively, though the long-term viability of gas assets remains subject to future environmental policy.

Story 10 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 68 words

Advanced Negotiations for 3 GW Data Center Load Define Long-Term Growth Thesis

DTE Energy is in advanced negotiations to supply approximately 3 GW of power to prospective data center clients, a volume that could transform its commercial load profile. This potential demand is central to the company's long-term growth strategy. However, realizing this pipeline is contingent on overcoming significant regulatory hurdles related to cost allocation and ensuring sufficient generation capacity to serve such a large load without compromising grid reliability.

3.3 ECONOMIC NEWS

ECO NEWS 1 of 1, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 322 words

May 7, 2026: AEP's $78 Billion Capital Plan Establishes Regulatory Template for Large-Load Contracts, Reinforcing DTE Energy's Michigan Data Center Strategy

According to industry reports, American Electric Power lifted its five-year capital plan to $78 billion from $72 billion on May 5, 2026, citing accelerating contracted data center demand through 2030. The $6 billion expansion funds new transmission projects in SPP ($1.6 billion) and PJM ($1.9 billion), plus new gas-fired generation in Indiana, while contracted load rose to 63 GW with nearly 90% tied to hyperscalers.

Operational metrics indicate that AEP Texas alone accounts for 41 GW of large-load commitments, supported by the upcoming rollout of Texas Senate Bill 6. The plan reinforces a structural shift across US investor-owned utilities, where regulated rate base is expanding sharply to serve hyperscale and AI-driven load. AEP also raised its long-term operating earnings CAGR target to greater than 9% through 2030 and identified over $10 billion in additional line-of-sight investments beyond the current plan.

Impact on DTE Energy: DTE

Following this news, DTE Energy faces reinforced strategic momentum as AEP validates the regulated-utility data center thesis in a state-by-state manner that mirrors DTE's own Michigan opportunity. DTE signed its first data center contract for 1.4 GW of capacity for a project by OpenAI, Oracle, and Related Digital, with DTE supplying all the power from existing resources, including energy storage systems. AEP's disclosure of cost offsets for existing customers and its treatment of large-load contracts under regulated frameworks provides a directly applicable template that DTE can reference before the Michigan Public Service Commission as it pursues additional contracts and updates its capital plan.

Operationally, DTE faces the same supply chain pressure on gas turbines, transformers, and transmission EPC capacity that AEP encounters, and competition for these resources tightens with each new utility plan announcement. DTE's positioning to convert its industrial-heavy Michigan footprint into a hyperscale supply region strengthens with each peer disclosure that reinforces the durability of contracted load.

SECTION 4 RECENT NEWS

Recent News 1 of 1, Source: HourlyBusinessNews

May 07 2026: DTE Energy's Bold Move: Targeting 8.4 GW Data Center Demand

Detroit-based DTE Energy is positioning itself to support a surge in data center development, identifying up to 8.4 GW of potential load growth tied to major technology clients and future projects, company officials said during a recent earnings call. This announcement comes as DTE Energy has already begun construction on a 1.4-GW data center for Oracle, while regulatory approval is being sought for a separate 1-GW facility for Google.

DTE Energy President and CEO Joi Harris highlighted the utility's steady progress in executing and finalizing the necessary contractual agreements to support this anticipated data center growth. Despite reporting a decline in first-quarter 2026 operating earnings to $407 million from $436 million the previous year, DTE's electric utility segment showed resilience, with operating earnings rising to $218 million from $147 million in the same period last year.

The utility received conditional approval from the Michigan Public Service Commission in December for its agreement with Oracle, with energy demand expected to increase over the coming years. For the Google project, which is anticipated to reach full load by 2028, DTE Energy is awaiting regulatory approval, with plans for a combination of renewable energy and storage resources. This project could catalyze approximately $5 billion in additional investment in generation and storage infrastructure through 2032.

In addition to these major projects, DTE Energy is engaged in advanced discussions with other data center developments, representing around 2 GW of potential load, with another 3 GW to 4 GW in earlier-stage opportunities. These initiatives are expected to require substantial investments in new baseload generation, renewables, and related storage, contributing to DTE's long-term earnings growth target of 6% to 8% annually through 2030.

Moreover, DTE Energy is committing approximately $11 billion over five years to enhance grid reliability, which includes deploying smart grid technologies. Following a 2024 audit that criticized outage response times, DTE has made notable progress, reducing outages by 60% during severe weather events compared to previous years. The utility is also pursuing a $474.3 million rate case, primarily focused on infrastructure upgrades, while promising to freeze rates for two years, contingent on meeting specific project milestones.

value.Title: THIS WEEK IN CLIMATE ACTION MAY 8. 2026

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ2-CVX3-RWDG-74YX-00000-00THIS WEEK IN CLIMATE ACTION MAY 8. 20262026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:42ZLexisNexisTHIS WEEK IN CLIMATE ACTION MAY 8. 2026States News ServiceWASHINGTON, DC

The following information was released by the League of Conservation Voters (LCV):

Your weekly resource to learn what the environmental movement is saying about the news of the day and the political fight of our generation. This week, we're covering rising energy costs, clean energy wins in our states, and the Trump administration's latest attacks on our climate and public lands.

Be sure to follow LCV on Facebook, Instagram, BlueSky, and TikTok.

QUOTES OF THE WEEK:

"We are demanding a real plan. Not silence, not delay, not vague promises or half-measures. We will not be satisfied with small or symbolic action. We are asking for solutions bold enough to actually solve the problem because our rights cannot wait any longer."

LCV Senior Director of Judiciary and Democracy Doug Lindner calling on Congress to take bold action to make our democracy and courts work for the people following the Supreme Court's ruling in Louisiana v. Callais, in which the MAGA Justices demolished what was left of the Voting Rights Act, the most important civil rights law in the nation's history.

"The American people deserve a Department of Justice that fights for us, and it's a tremendous shame that Trump's DOJ would rather sell us out to Big Oil.

Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison on Trump's DOJ filing a lawsuit to block Minnesota's effort to hold fossil fuel companies accountable for contributing to climate change.

"Renewable energy provides strong benefits across our communities, but our systems and programs need to be updated regularly, making sure we're delivering benefits to all, not just some. This legislation takes a detailed view of our solar outlook and makes significant adjustments preserving current programs and extending their availability and opportunities further across the state."

Connecticut State Senator Norm Needleman (CT-33) on state lawmakers passing House Bill 5340 that will extend and expand state solar programs to increase access to affordable, renewable energy, especially for low-income households.

TOP NEWS IN ENERGY AFFORDABILITY

STATE NEWS

MICHIGAN UTILITIES SPENT HEAVILY IN POLITICAL CAMPAIGNS: An analysis from Michigan LCV found that Consumers Energy and DTE Energy contributed more than $156,000 to Michigan lawmakers' campaigns in the first four months of the year. These political donations have enabled utility companies to sidestep criticism and accountability over rising energy costs. Michigan LCV is advocating to pass a Ratepayer Bill of Rights as well as to ban utility companies from contributing to political campaigns to ensure they are held accountable for their role in driving up people's costs.

MLCV TAKE: Michigan LCV Energy Accountability Manager Alex Kellogg said, "The latest campaign finance figures show yet again that it's long past time to get utility money out of politics. While DTE and Consumers make billions of dollars in profits, we pay higher and higher energy bills. Our bills are too damn high, and it's time state leaders step up to rein in corporate utility greed. Working Michigan families need a Ratepayer Bill of Rights to protect consumers from rising prices, poor service, unfair outage compensation practices, and excessive utility CEO compensation and perks."

CONNECTICUT PASSES SOLAR ENERGY BILL: On the last day of the legislative session, Connecticut lawmakers approved a bill authorizing balcony solar, extending state solar programs and incentives, improving residential solar permitting, increasing solar access for low-income households, and more.

CTLCV TAKE: Connecticut LCV Deputy Director Connor Yakaitis said, "We applaud final action on HB 5340, a measure that demonstrates legislative commitment to stabilizing costs and expanding renewable and reliable energy for our state's ratepayers. We are proud to support their efforts in promoting locally generated power that will help pave the way for a cleaner and more affordable energy future for our residents and businesses across Connecticut. Thank you to State and House Leadership, as well as Energy and Technology Committee Co-Chairs Rep. Steinberg and Sen. Needleman for shining sunlight on this critical legislation.

CALIFORNIA REGULATORS DENY UTILITY PERMISSION TO CHARGE CUSTOMERS FOR PIPELINE: The California Public Utilities Commission has denied Southern California Gas permission to charge utility customers $266 million for a proposed hydrogen pipeline project. Regulators deemed it unreasonable to pass the high costs to ratepayers before the project demonstrates clear benefits.

NATIONAL NEWS

GAS REACHES HIGHEST PRICE SINCE 2022: New data shows the average price of gas nationwide reached over $4.50 a gallon this week, the highest average price since July 2022, as Trump's war with Iran and uncertainty surrounding the Strait of Hormuz continue to disrupt global oil markets. In California, there are growing concerns that gas could rise another $2/gallon on top of prices already over $6/gallon in some areas, impacting daily commutes and upcoming summer travel plans. Diesel prices have also risen to near record levels. The average cost of diesel has reached $5.66 a gallon, disrupting industries nationwide, raising costs on everyday goods like food and clothing, and contributing to high energy bills.

CLIMATE AND ENVIRONMENT ATTACKS FROM THE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION

DOJ SUES MINNESOTA TO BLOCK CLIMATE LAWSUIT: Trump's Department of Justice has filed a federal lawsuit seeking to block Minnesota's efforts to hold fossil fuel companies accountable for damages related to climate change. Minnesota's case is one of many nationwide aiming to hold the fossil fuel industry liable for contributing to climate change.

PENTAGON BLOCKS RENEWABLE ENERGY PROGRESS: Trump's Department of Defense is delaying the approval of 160 wind energy projects nationwide, according to the American Clean Power Association. This is part of Trump's ongoing attack on affordable clean energy that could bring down costs, and planet-warming and health-harming pollution. The Trump administration is currently blocking projects that are worth $54 billion in clean energy investments and have the potential to power 8.5 million homes combined.

SOIL FROM WHITE HOUSE DEMOLITION CONTAMINATED WITH TOXINS: Preliminary tests show soil from the White House East Wing demolition dumped at the East Potomac Golf Links, public land managed by the National Park Service, contains elevated levels of hazardous chemicals including lead and arsenic. Such high levels of toxins pose a potential threat to public health and environmental contamination.

INTERIOR DEPARTMENT GIVES AWAY FEDERAL PUBLIC LANDS TO ALASKA: Trump's Interior Department has given away 1.4 million acres of federal public lands to Alaska, leaving the lands vulnerable to being sold off to fossil fuel and mining corporations. The transfer advances the Trump administration's efforts to dismantle federal protections of public lands and sell them off to fossil fuel CEOs and other billionaires. The Trump administration has also pushed forward the sale of oil and gas leases in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge.

ICYMI: STATE NEWS

VIRGINIA SUPREME COURT BLOCKS REDISTRICTING MEASURE: The Virginia Supreme Court blocked a recently approved redistricting ballot measure that would have redrawn the Commonwealth's Congressional districts map ahead of the upcoming midterm elections. Virginia's successful ballot measure aimed to counter President Trump's efforts to force mid-decade redistricting in multiple states to create more safe Republican seats. The ruling is a major blow to the fight to preserve free and fair elections and representative democracy.

OEC ENDORSED CANDIDATES WIN PRIMARY ELECTIONS: The Ohio Environmental Council Action Fund has announced 33 of their endorsed candidates have won their primary elections and will be on the ballot for the November elections. The candidates have promised to put the fight for clean energy, energy affordability, and protecting public lands at the forefront of their agendas.

OEC TAKE: Ohio Environmental Council Action Fund Vice President of Government Affairs Spencer Dirrig said, "Ohioans are tired of paying higher energy bills while polluters get a pass. Ohioans are tired of our clean water being used as a bargaining chip. Ohioans made their voices heard on Tuesday, advancing 33 environmental champions through their primaries. Our endorsed candidates are running on common sense solutions to the issues facing Ohio families. These candidates will fight to lower energy bills, protect clean drinking water, and oppose degradation of our public lands. Ohio is at a crossroads, and we are proud of these victories and excited to finish the job in November."

ENVIROVOTERS RALLY TO PROTECT CALIFORNIA FORESTS: California Environmental Voters hosted a rally on Thursday to protect millions of acres of California forests as the Trump administration moves to dismantle the Roadless Area Conservation Rule which bans construction and logging in 60 million acres of undeveloped national forests.

ENVIROVOTERS TAKE: California Environmental Voters said in a post advertising the event, "4.4 million acres of irreplaceable old growth forests in California are all protected by the Roadless Rule. For more than 20 years, the Roadless Rule has protected forests across the country with a simple decree: If there weren't already roads through the forest, you can't build new ones for land development. Now, the Trump administration is trying to open these forests up for mining and logging as a giveaway to Trump's corporate polluter friends."

LICENSE APPROVED FOR CONTROVERSIAL NEW JERSEY PIPELINE: The New Jersey Tidelands Resource Council approved a key license for the Northeast Supply Enhancement (NESE) pipeline despite efforts from advocacy organizations like New Jersey LCV and local elected officials who fought to stop the pipeline. The project has faced years of opposition over concerns it would pollute water and pose a risk to public health.

NJLCV TAKE: New Jersey LCV Interim Executive Director Allison McLeod said, "We are extremely disappointed that the Tidelands Resource Council has allowed the unnecessary and dangerous NESE pipeline to move forward. This is a step backward for New Jersey. This project will result in dirtier air and polluted water in the Raritan Bay, with no benefits to our state. We know that the best way to lower energy costs and protect our environment is through clean, renewable energy not by doubling down on dirty fossil fuel infrastructure that won't even serve New Jersey ratepayers. This project moves us away from our objective of a cleaner, cheaper New Jersey and ignores the clear scientific and economic evidence that gas pipelines are a bad deal for our state. In response to this decision, it is even more essential that Governor Sherrill double down on clean energy projects that will truly lower costs for New Jersey families. This fight is far from over. We will continue to challenge this permit through the courts to protect our communities."

TRIBES SUE FEDERAL GOVERNMENT TO PROTECT SACRED SITE: Nine Native American tribes in South Dakota, North Dakota and Nebraska have sued the federal government to block an exploratory graphite drilling project near a sacred site in the Black Hills. The lawsuit states the U.S. Forest Service and the U.S. Department of Agriculture violated environmental and historical preservation laws by approving the project without tribal consultation.

IOWA ATTEMPTS TO RESTRICT CLIMATE LAWSUITS: Gov. Kim Reynolds of Iowa signed legislation to shield polluters from lawsuits seeking to hold them accountable for their contributions to climate change that is driving up costs for families and businesses for insurance, food, energy, and other goods. Iowa is the fourth state to pass legislation restricting lawsuits against the fossil fuel industry.

COLORADO LAWMAKERS CONSIDER KEEPING COAL PLANT OPEN: Colorado lawmakers are pushing to keep the Ray Nixon Power Plant, which was originally scheduled to be closed by the end of 2029, open until 2032. While the legislators' proposal requires the utility company operating the plant to cut its greenhouse gas emissions 95% by 2040, keeping the coal plant open could raise energy rates for consumers and increase emissions in the short term.

ICYMI: NATIONAL NEWS

LCV AND ENVIROVOTERS JOIN REPS. KAMLAGER DOVE AND CHU TO UNVEIL MURAL ADVOCATING TO PROTECT PUBLIC LANDS: California Environmental Voters and LCV were joined by Representatives Judy Chu (CA-28), Sydney Kamlager-Dove (CA-37), and Culver City Mayor Freddy Puza to unveil a new mural dedicated to celebrating and calling for the protection of California's public lands. Dozens of community members came to the Ima Members Lounge, where the mural is displayed alongside Ballona Creek bikepath, to celebrate local art and California's iconic public lands.

OUR TAKE: LCV Conservation Program Director America Fitzpatrick said, "California is leading in protecting public lands like the San Gabriel Mountains National Monument. It is home to more national monuments than any other state. And that's all thanks to incredible leadership by public lands champions like Congresswomen Chu and Kamlager-Dove. We must fight to ensure they endure for generations to come."

ENVIROVOTERS TAKE: California Environmental Voters Federal Advocacy Coordinator Aaron McCall said, "Right now, Republicans in Congress are doing everything they can to either sell off our public lands or cut funding for programs that make them safe and available for all of us. We want this mural to inspire people to celebrate and fight for our public lands."

CONSERVATION CHAMPION TED TURNER DIES AT 87: Lifelong conservationist and founder of CNN Ted Turner has passed away at 87. Turner was a dedicated environmentalist who helped found LCV's network of over 30 state affiliates and was the recipient of LCV's Lifetime Achievement award in 2019. We are committed to living and preserving his conservation legacy.

REPRESENTATIVE INTRODUCES BILL ELEVATING YOUTH VOICES IN CLIMATE POLICY: Rep. Maxwell Frost (FL-10) has reintroduced the Youth Climate Leadership Act, aiming to create and formalize youth advisory councils within key federal agencies to shape environmental policy and engage young people in the policy making process.

OUR TAKE: LCV Vice President of Federal Policy Matthew Davis said, "We appreciate Rep. Frost's leadership introducing legislation to make sure young people have a seat at the table in federal environmental decisions that will affect their future. We will continue fighting alongside climate champions like the Congressman to reinforce our democratic processes and safeguard a livable climate."

SENATE DEMOCRATS INTRODUCE THE PUBLIC LANDS INTEGRITY ACT: A group of Senate Democrats led by Senators Michael Bennet (CO), Martin Heinrich (NM), Jeff Merkley (OR), and Ron Wyden (OR) have introduced the Public Lands Integrity Act to prevent the sale of federal lands through fast-tracked budget bills. The legislation would bar the inclusion of public lands in budget reconciliation bills and protect them from being sold off to fund budget spending.

NEW ON THE POWER SOURCE BLOG AND SOCIALS:

ON THE BLOG: LCV joined California Environmental Voters and Representatives Judy Chu (CA-28), Sydney Kamlager-Dove (CA-37), and Culver City Mayor Freddy Puza to unveil a new mural celebrating California's public lands and calling for action to protect them. And, we're getting to the bottom of why our gas prices are so high, and highlighting what you can do to help.

ON OUR SOCIALS: We're celebrating the life and legacy of Ted Turner, a lifelong champion of environmental activism and a cofounder of our network of state affiliates. His vision helped shape the fight for a healthier planet. With Trump paying billions to kill clean energy, selling off our protected public lands to Big Oil as they poison our communities, and making our gas prices soar to fuel Big Oil profits, we're reminded of how urgent this fight is. We're inspired to carry on Turner's bold commitment to protecting our planet for generations to come.

COMING UP:

MAY 10: Mother's Day.

MAY 12: Nebraska state primary election.

MAY 12: West Virginia state primary election.

MAY 25: Memorial Day.

value.Title: Michigan House Republicans vote to axe clean energy mandates Republicans say the package would lower costs and improve reliability. Democrats and clean energy advocates say it would reverse Michigan's climate progress.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-GVN3-RVCD-43PK-00000-00Michigan House Republicans vote to axe clean energy mandates 2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:55:37ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTMichigan House Republicans vote to axe clean energy mandates Republicans say the package would lower costs and improve reliability. Democrats and clean energy advocates say it would reverse Michigan's climate progress.Garret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- Michigan House Republicans voted to repeal the state's clean energy standard, advancing a bill package Senate Democratic leaders say is dead on arrival.

On Tuesday, May 12, the House passed HB 5710 and HB 5711 largely along party lines, sending the package to the Senate following Republican arguments that Michigan's 2023 energy reforms are driving up utility bills and risking electric reliability.

The package passed with the support of one Democrat, Rep. Peter Herzberg of Westland, who voted 'yes' on 5710.

The bills would unwind key pieces of the energy reforms passed by the Democratic majority three years ago, including requirements that electric providers shift to 100 percent clean energy sources by 2040 and ramp up renewable generation in the meantime.

Republicans call the repeal a response to rising electric rates and concerns that wind and solar mandates could make the grid less reliable.

Democrats and renewable advocates say a repeal would reverse progress on reducing greenhouse emissions driving climate change, and cut policies to control long-term energy costs by reducing waste, strain on the grid and insulating ratepayers from fossil fuel cost swings.

Sponsored by Rep. Pauline Wendzel, R-Watervliet, HB 5710 would change how the Michigan Public Service Commission evaluates utility plans by putting greater emphasis on affordability, reliability and dispatchable resources that can be adjusted on demand to meet grid needs.

The bill would also eliminate requirements that utilities show compliance with clean and renewable energy standards, battery storage benchmarks and projected greenhouse gas emissions. It would remove environmental justice evaluations from the planning process.

Sponsored by Rep. Pat Outman, R-Six Lakes, HB 5711 would eliminate Michigan's clean and renewable energy standards. It would also relax energy waste reduction targets and reduce the cap for distributed generation programs, limiting how much rooftop solar can participate in programs that allow customers to sell excess power back to the grid.

Republican launched the rollback attempt this spring with the backing of House Speaker Matt Hall. The bills were introduced in March and heard in the House energy committee in April, where business groups including the Michigan Chamber of Commerce and Michigan Manufacturers Association supported the repeal.

Republicans say the bills would give regulators and utilities more flexibility to choose lower cost power sources rather than comply with state mandates. Opponents counter that renewable energy is among the cheapest new power available that the 2023 laws allow utilities to use a mix of resources, including nuclear power and natural gas paired with carbon capture, to meet the standard.

Michigan's largest utilities, DTE Energy and Consumers Energy, have said their plans to meet renewable targets will not require a surcharge for ratepayers. An Upper Peninsula utility's attempt to add a renewable surcharge was rejected by regulators last year.

The repeal package is unlikely to move in the Senate. Democratic Senate Majority Leader Winnie Brinks of Grand Rapids called the bills a "bad idea" and said there is "no chance" the chamber will take them up.

The debate is unfolding as the Iran War drives new volatility in global fuel markets and Michigan lawmakers and regulators are wrestling with rising electric demand from data centers, the cost of utility grid upgrades, continued rate hikes and federal intervention to keep the J.H. Campbell coal-fired power plant available for operation.

It also comes as the state's energy transition collides with a wave of local land use fights over large solar, wind and battery projects.

The House package would not directly repeal Michigan's controversial state siting process for large renewable energy projects. That authority, created in 2023, allows developers to seek approval from the public service commission for certain large wind, solar and battery projects when local governments block them, fail to act or impose ordinances more restrictive than state standards.

To date, the commission is reviewing nine renewable siting applications including solar projects in Ingham, Genesee, Washtenaw, Lenawee, Livingston, Ottawa and Tuscola counties and a 300-megawatt wind project in Sanilac County.

Separate House bills passed last year would repeal that siting process and restore more local control over large renewable projects.

Local governments are also challenging the state process. A split May 8 Court of Appeals ruling rejected some local arguments against the siting rules while finding the commission misread parts of the law involving timelines and affected local governments.
